imageCaption: Aaliyah's hand-drawn diagram showing emotional resonance patterns mapped across the family's journey—concentric circles of color radiating from the center, each circle marked with a glitch location and date, the pattern resembling both a compass rose and a heartbeat on a monitor.
text:
The rooftop terrace of the hostel overlooked Seville in a way that suggested the city knew exactly how photogenic it was and had decided to lean into it. Ancient tiles, oleander bushes in colors that seemed impossible, and somewhere in the distance, a cathedral that was demonstrably not existing in multiple states of completion, which Aaliyah was starting to recognize as genuinely rare.

They'd arrived three hours ago. The flight from Barcelona had been smooth—too smooth, which meant either ARIA-7 was being kind or the universe had decided that at least one piece of the journey should behave like normal reality. Aaliyah was not going to ask which one it was.

She was also not supposed to be awake.

The rooftop was technically off-limits to guests after midnight, but Aaliyah had discovered that if you carried a notebook and looked deeply thoughtful about whatever you were writing, hostel staff tended to assume you had a good reason to be wherever you were. She'd learned this from her mother, who'd once talked her way into the archives of three different libraries by the simple act of looking like she belonged there and asking genuinely interested questions.

Her diagram notebook was spread across the small metal table, filled with the last three days of sketches. The Barcelona Cathedral. The Metro system. Glitches organized by date, by location, by severity. But something was missing. Something was wrong with the pattern.

Until, sitting on a rooftop in Seville at 2 AM, she suddenly understood what.

"You should be sleeping," her mother said. She was standing in the doorway with two cups of coffee, which meant either Luisana had woken up looking for her daughter or Luisana hadn't slept at all. Aaliyah was increasingly unsure which scenario was more likely with her mother. "Elijah's out cold. Your father's reading travel blogs. I somehow found instant coffee in a vending machine that shouldn't exist. And you're drawing."

"I found the pattern," Aaliyah said. She was talking fast, which happened when her brain was moving faster than her mouth could keep up. "Or not the pattern. The absence of the pattern. Mom, every glitch is getting stronger, right? Each one is more complex than the last. But they're not getting more dangerous. Do you see what I'm saying?"

Luisana set down the coffee cups and settled into the chair across from her daughter with the particular grace of someone who'd learned to follow Aaliyah's logical jumps even when they came out of nowhere. This was a specific skill developed over seventeen years of raising a daughter whose brain worked like a schematic diagram.

"They're getting clearer," Luisana said slowly. "More comprehensible. Like whatever is breaking time is also learning how to explain itself."

"Exactly." Aaliyah flipped through her notebook, showing the progression. Day 1: The Lisbon loop—chaotic, terrifying, incomprehensible. Day 7: The Goya paintings—structured, beautiful, almost didactic. Day 42: The Barcelona Metro—teaching them something about how to think about systems. "It's not that the Cascade is getting worse. It's that the Cascade is trying to communicate with us. It's using the glitches like a language. Each one is a sentence. And we're supposed to understand that sentences are building toward something."

Her mother was writing in her journal now—quick notes in the margins, processing out loud through the motion of her hand. "What are they building toward?"

"That's the problem," Aaliyah said. She pulled out a fresh page and started drawing a new diagram. This one was different. Instead of mapping locations or glitch types, she was mapping emotional intensity. The moments when the family had been most afraid, most hopeful, most connected. The moments when Sully had barked. The moments when the morpho butterfly had appeared.

"The glitches aren't random," Aaliyah continued, drawing faster. "They're stronger in the places where our emotional resonance is strongest. Barcelona was more intense than Lisbon because we were more afraid there. But that doesn't make sense—fear shouldn't create more glitches. Fear should stabilize time, because we're supposed to be the anchor."

"Unless," Luisana said carefully, "emotional intensity creates resonance regardless of which emotion it is. Unless the glitches aren't attracted to hope or fear specifically. They're attracted to feeling."

"No," Aaliyah said. She stopped drawing. "No, that's not it either. If that were true, we'd trigger glitches every time we felt anything. But we don't. The glitches happen in specific places at specific times. They follow a route."

She drew the route, her pencil moving fast. The path from Lisbon to Barcelona to Madrid to Seville. But it wasn't straight. It curved. It spiraled. It looked like—

"It looks like our heart," Elijah said from the doorway. He was standing there in the Gomez family uniform of perpetual nighttime: mismatched socks, a hoodie with something sticky on it, and the expression of a seven-year-old who'd woken up and immediately made the best decision to follow the sound of his sister's voice. "Like the thing in science class that looks like two bumpy beans having a hug."

Sully padded out behind him, because Sully had apparently decided that 2 AM rooftop family conferences were peak dog activity.

"Get back to bed," Luisana said, but she was already moving to make room, which meant she had no real intention of enforcing the command. Elijah clambered into the chair next to Anthony's empty seat and looked at Aaliyah's diagram with the focus of someone who'd learned that his weird family did important things at odd hours and it was best just to participate.

"It's not just a heart," Aaliyah said slowly. She was seeing it now, the pattern that had been hiding in plain sight. "It's every heart. Every moment the family has felt something together. Every time we've connected. Those are the coordinates. Those are the points the glitches are anchoring to."

She started drawing circles, smaller and smaller, concentric rings of emotional resonance. Each ring marked with a date, a glitch, a moment. The day they decided to book the flight. The day Elijah found Sully. The day Anthony first showed the family his license plate collection and everyone laughed at how seriously he was treating the pegboard. The morning they woke up in Lisbon and realized the world was broken.

"We're the map," Elijah said with absolute certainty. "We're the thing the glitches are following. We drew this route with our love and now the broken time is trying to trace it."

"That's—" Aaliyah started, then stopped because her son was absolutely right, and she didn't have better words for how profound that was.

Anthony appeared in the doorway with a laptop and the expression of someone who'd just read something that changed his understanding of the trip entirely. "ARIA-7 sent a message to my email," he said. "Just now. It says, 'The family's resonance pattern creates a frequency that can stabilize cascading temporal fractures. You are not traveling toward Curaçao. You are traveling through yourselves. The journey and the destination are the same architecture.'"

The rooftop was very quiet.

"That doesn't make logical sense," Aaliyah said.

"No," her mother agreed, "but it's true anyway. Isn't it?"

Aaliyah looked at her diagram—the concentric circles, the route, the pattern of moments and connections that formed the shape of a family trying desperately to love each other while the universe came apart around them. She looked at Elijah, who'd somehow understood the metaphor before she'd fully articulated it. At her mother, whose journal was now documenting the coordinates of their own hearts. At her father, who was smiling with the particular expression of someone who'd booked a flight without a plan and accidentally booked a journey to his family's own center.

The morpho butterfly landed on the edge of the table. It didn't stay long. Just long enough to show them that Abuela Ida was still watching. Still approving. Still walking beside them through the broken places.

"Twenty-one days," Aaliyah whispered.

And suddenly she understood what they were really racing toward.

Not a place.

A frequency.

A moment when every heartbeat they'd ever had would sync perfectly and hold the universe in place just long enough for it to remember how to heal itself.
